section.title: Saul Rejected as King
section.sectionRationale: God commands Saul to devote the Amalekites entirely to destruction in judgment. Saul wins the battle but spares King Agag and the best of the animals, then claims he obeyed and blames the troops, saying the best was kept to sacrifice. Samuel confronts him: obedience is better than sacrifice, and because Saul rejected the word of the LORD, the LORD has rejected him as king. The kingdom is torn away and given to a better neighbor. It is one movement: the final rejection of Saul, and the great lesson that God desires obedience above religion.
section.thesis: Saul's partial obedience is exposed as full disobedience dressed in religious excuses, and God, who prizes obedience above sacrifice and does not lie or change His mind, tears the kingdom from Saul and gives it to a better king, teaching that to reject God's word is to be rejected, and pointing to the obedient King whose throne endures.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the command against Amalek and Saul's partial obedience, verses 1 to 9; the LORD's regret and Samuel's confrontation, verses 10 to 21; obedience better than sacrifice and the sentence, verses 22 to 26; the torn robe and torn kingdom, verses 27 to 31; and the end of Agag and of Samuel's visits, verses 32 to 35.

units.label: The command against Amalek and Saul's partial obedience
units.note: they were unwilling to devote them to destruction

verses.text: Then Samuel said to Saul, “The LORD sent me to anoint you king over His people Israel. Now therefore, listen to the words of the LORD.
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘I witnessed what the Amalekites did to the Israelites when they opposed them on their way up from Egypt.

Now go and attack the Amalekites and {{cherem|devote to destruction}} all that belongs to them. Do not spare them, but put to death men and women, children and infants, oxen and sheep, camels and donkeys.’”
So Saul summoned the troops and numbered them at Telaim, 200,000 foot soldiers and 10,000 men of Judah.
Saul came to the city of Amalek and lay in wait in the valley.

And he warned the Kenites, “Since you showed kindness to all the Israelites when they came up out of Egypt, go on and get away from the Amalekites. Otherwise I will sweep you away with them.” So the Kenites moved away from the Amalekites.
Then Saul struck down the Amalekites all the way from Havilah to Shur, which is east of Egypt.
He captured Agag king of Amalek alive, but devoted all the others to destruction with the sword.

Saul and his troops spared Agag, along with the best of the sheep and cattle, the fat calves and lambs, and the best of everything else. They were unwilling to devote them to destruction, but they devoted to destruction all that was despised and worthless.

units.label: The LORD's regret and Samuel's confrontation
units.note: I regret that I have made Saul king

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Samuel, saying,
“I regret that I have made Saul king, for he has turned away from following Me and has not carried out My instructions.” And Samuel was distressed and cried out to the LORD all that night.
Early in the morning Samuel got up to confront Saul, but he was told, “Saul has gone to Carmel, and behold, he has set up a monument for himself and has turned and gone down to Gilgal.”
When Samuel reached him, Saul said to him, “May the LORD bless you. I have carried out the LORD’s instructions.”
But Samuel replied, “Then what is this bleating of sheep and lowing of cattle that I hear?”
Saul answered, “The troops brought them from the Amalekites; they spared the best sheep and cattle to sacrifice to the LORD your God, but the rest we devoted to destruction.”
“Stop!” exclaimed Samuel. “Let me tell you what the LORD said to me last night.” “Tell me,” Saul replied.
And Samuel said, “Although you were once small in your own eyes, have you not become the head of the tribes of Israel? The LORD anointed you king over Israel
and sent you on a mission, saying, ‘Go and devote to destruction the sinful Amalekites. Fight against them until you have wiped them out.’
So why did you not obey the LORD? Why did you rush upon the plunder and do evil in the sight of the LORD?”
“But I did obey the LORD,” Saul replied. “I went on the mission that the LORD gave me. I brought back Agag king of Amalek and devoted the Amalekites to destruction.
The troops took sheep and cattle from the plunder, the best of the things devoted to destruction, in order to sacrifice them to the LORD your God at Gilgal.”

units.label: Obedience better than sacrifice and the sentence
units.note: to obey is better than sacrifice

verses.text: But Samuel declared: “Does the LORD delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as much as in {{shama|obedience}} to His voice? Behold, obedience is better than sacrifice, and attentiveness is better than the fat of rams.
For rebellion is like the sin of divination, and arrogance is like the wickedness of idolatry. Because you have {{maas|rejected}} the word of the LORD, He has rejected you as king.”
Then Saul said to Samuel, “I have sinned; I have transgressed the LORD’s commandment and your instructions, because I feared the people and obeyed their voice.
Now therefore, please forgive my sin and return with me so I can worship the LORD.”
“I will not return with you,” Samuel replied. “For you have rejected the word of the LORD, and He has rejected you as king over Israel.”

units.label: The torn robe and torn kingdom
units.note: the LORD has torn the kingdom from you

verses.text: As Samuel turned to go, Saul grabbed the hem of his robe, and it tore.
So Samuel said to him, “The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to your neighbor who is better than you.
Moreover, the Glory of Israel does not lie or change His mind, for He is not a man, that He should change His mind.”
“I have sinned,” Saul replied. “Please honor me now before the elders of my people and before Israel. Come back with me, so that I may worship the LORD your God.”
So Samuel went back with Saul, and Saul worshiped the LORD.

units.label: The end of Agag and of Samuel's visits
units.note: the LORD regretted that He had made Saul king

verses.text: Then Samuel said, “Bring me Agag king of the Amalekites.” Agag came to him cheerfully, for he thought, “Surely the bitterness of death is past.”
But Samuel declared: “As your sword has made women childless, so your mother will be childless among women.” And Samuel hacked Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.
Then Samuel went to Ramah, but Saul went up to his home in Gibeah of Saul.
And to the day of his death, Samuel never again visited Saul. Samuel mourned for Saul, and the LORD regretted that He had made Saul king over Israel.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted to destruction or given over wholly to the LORD, whether by destroying it or setting it apart to Him. In holy war against a people ripe for judgment, the cherem meant nothing was to be taken as personal plunder; all belonged to God. Saul's keeping the best for himself, then claiming it was for sacrifice, was a direct violation of what was wholly the LORD's.

shama.gloss: To hear, listen, obey. Samuel's verdict, to obey is better than sacrifice, uses the word that opens the great command, Hear, O Israel. True hearing of God is obedience; Saul offered religious activity, sacrifice, in place of the listening obedience God desires. God wants a heart that heeds His voice more than any offering laid on His altar.

maas.gloss: To reject, refuse, despise. The word turns back on Saul with terrible symmetry: because you have rejected the word of the LORD, He has rejected you as king. To spurn God's command is to be spurned from God's service. The same verb described Israel rejecting God's kingship in chapter 8; now it falls on the king they demanded.

basic.context: God gives Saul a clear command: carry out His long-promised judgment on the Amalekites and devote everything to destruction, keeping no plunder. Saul defeats them but spares their king, Agag, and the best of the animals. When Samuel comes, Saul greets him claiming, I have carried out the LORD's instructions, until the bleating sheep give him away. He blames the soldiers and says the best was kept back to sacrifice to God. Samuel cuts through it: to obey is better than sacrifice; because you have rejected the word of the LORD, He has rejected you as king. As Samuel turns to leave, Saul tears his robe, and Samuel says God has torn the kingdom from Saul and given it to a better man. The question is: does God want our religion or our obedience? He desires a listening, obedient heart above all sacrifices.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative carrying one of the sharpest lessons in Scripture: partial obedience is disobedience, and religious activity is no substitute for obeying God. The command to devote Amalek to destruction belongs to a specific moment of divine judgment on a people, executed through Israel's king; it is not a pattern for believers, who are told to love their enemies. We must read Saul's excuses honestly, seeing how self-justification and blame-shifting mask rebellion. The enduring truths are that God prizes obedience over sacrifice and that rejecting His word brings rejection.
basic.originalLanguages: The command was to place Amalek under cherem, wholly devoted to the LORD, so keeping the best as plunder was theft from God, however piously excused. Samuel's verdict rests on shama, to hear and obey, the word of the great command Hear, O Israel: to obey is better than sacrifice. And the sentence turns on maas, to reject: because Saul rejected the word of the LORD, the LORD rejected him as king, the same word that described Israel rejecting God's rule in chapter 8.
basic.typology: The rejected, disobedient king points by contrast to the obedient King, Christ, who did not offer religion in place of obedience but obeyed the Father perfectly, even to death. The kingdom torn from Saul and given to a better neighbor traces the line to David and finally to Jesus, the King who cannot be rejected. The word that obedience is better than sacrifice finds its deepest answer in the One whose obedience is the true and acceptable sacrifice.
basic.christ: Samuel says God has given the kingdom to your neighbor who is better than you. That better neighbor is David, but David points beyond himself to Christ, the King who is truly better, whose obedience never fails. Where Saul substituted sacrifice for obedience, Jesus united them, offering the perfect obedience that is itself the acceptable sacrifice, He came to do the Father's will, and by that will we are saved. Saul is rejected for rejecting God's word; Christ is the beloved Son who always kept it, and so His kingdom endures forever. The tragedy of Saul makes us long for the King who obeys.
basic.god: God is holy and just, executing long-delayed judgment and requiring full obedience from His king. He sees through excuses and religious cover, valuing a listening heart above sacrifices. He is grieved by Saul's sin, so that the narrator can speak of His regret, yet He is also the Glory of Israel who does not lie or change His mind. He is sovereign, tearing the kingdom away and giving it to another, and faithful to His larger purpose even in judgment.
basic.crossReferences: Micah 6 asks what the LORD requires and answers with obedience and justice, not sacrifice. Hosea 6 says God desires mercy, not sacrifice. Hebrews 10 shows Christ coming to do God's will as the true offering. And Matthew 7 warns that not everyone who says Lord, Lord will enter, but those who do the Father's will.
basic.oneStory: Saul's final rejection turns on the great truth that God desires obedience above sacrifice. The kingdom is torn away and given to a better king, David, and through him the story runs to Christ, the obedient King whose kingdom endures. The rejected king makes way for the King after God's own heart, and finally for the Son who always does the Father's will.

context.literary: This chapter is the climax of Saul's decline and his decisive rejection, following the first warning in chapter 13. It ends the account of Saul's rise and fall and immediately precedes the anointing of David in chapter 16. The poetic oracle of verses 22 to 23 is the theological heart, and the torn robe, verses 27 to 28, provides a memorable enacted sign of the torn kingdom.
context.historical: The Amalekites had attacked Israel treacherously after the exodus, Exodus 17 and Deuteronomy 25, and stood under a standing sentence of judgment. The cherem, devotion to destruction, meant that in this holy war nothing was to be taken for personal gain; all belonged to God. Setting up a monument to himself, verse 12, reveals Saul's pride. Agag's line and the ongoing Amalekite threat surface again later, including Haman the Agagite in Esther.
context.canonical: The principle that obedience is better than sacrifice becomes a refrain of the prophets, Micah 6, Hosea 6, Amos 5, and is taken up by Jesus. The rejection of Saul and the giving of the kingdom to a better king set up the Davidic covenant and the messianic hope. The statement that God does not change His mind, alongside His regret, contributes to the Bible's rich portrait of God's constancy and His real engagement with human sin.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the final rejection of Saul for his disobedience and self-justifying religion, and to press home that God desires obedient hearing above all sacrifice, so that to reject God's word is to be rejected, while the kingdom passes to a better king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique command of judgment on Amalek and Saul's failure; it does not prescribe holy war for the church, which is called to love its enemies. What endures is that partial obedience is disobedience, that religious activity cannot substitute for obeying God, and that God prizes a listening, submitted heart.

debates.question: How do we understand the command to devote the Amalekites, including children, to destruction?

views.label: A unique act of divine judgment through Israel's king
views.case: The Amalekites stood under a specific, long-announced sentence for their treachery against Israel, Exodus 17 and Deuteronomy 25; this was God's judgment executed at a particular moment in redemptive history, not a general license for violence, and it belongs to God, the righteous Judge of all the earth, who alone has the right to give and take life.

views.label: A sign, in stark form, of sin's seriousness and God's holiness
views.case: The cherem dramatizes that persistent, corporate evil ripe for judgment cannot be tolerated before a holy God; while sobering, it points to the reality of divine judgment that the whole Bible affirms and that Christ Himself will finally execute, even as He now calls His people to love their enemies.

debates.bearing: Both stress that this is God's judgment in a unique redemptive moment, not a model for the church; it magnifies God's holiness and justice and drives us to the mercy found in Christ, who bore judgment for sinners.

debates.question: How can God regret making Saul king, verses 11 and 35, yet not change His mind, verse 29?

views.label: Real divine grief, not a reversal of purpose
views.case: God's regret expresses His genuine sorrow and moral response to Saul's sin, a true relational engagement, while verse 29 affirms that God does not lie or waver in His settled purposes; He is grieved by sin without being fickle or mistaken.

views.label: Human language describing God's consistent character
views.case: Scripture speaks of God's regret in terms we can grasp, yet His unchanging faithfulness stands; He consistently opposes disobedience, so His response to the disobedient Saul is not a change in God but the outworking of His constant holiness.

debates.bearing: Both hold that God is neither fickle nor detached: He truly responds to and grieves over sin, yet remains utterly faithful and unchanging in His character and purposes, a God we can both revere and trust.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the chapter's weight. The command placed Amalek under cherem, wholly devoted to the LORD, so that nothing was to be kept as personal plunder; all belonged to God as an act of His judgment. Saul's keeping the best of the flocks, then dressing it up as material for sacrifice, was therefore theft from God however piously excused. Against that excuse Samuel sets the word shama, to hear and to obey, the very word that opens Israel's great command, Hear, O Israel: does the LORD delight in sacrifices as much as in obeying His voice? To obey is better than sacrifice. True hearing of God is obedience, and no amount of religious offering can replace it. And the sentence falls with terrible symmetry on the word maas, to reject: because you have rejected the word of the LORD, He has rejected you as king. It is the same verb that named Israel's rejection of God's kingship in chapter 8; now it lands on the king they had demanded. To spurn God's word is to be spurned from His service.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the obedient King who kept the Father's word perfectly and so cannot be rejected, Philippians 2:8 and John 8:29.

typology.type: The kingdom given to a better neighbor
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom secured in David and finally in Christ, the King who is truly better, Luke 1:32-33.
Christ, whose perfect obedience is itself the true and acceptable sacrifice, Hebrews 10:5-10.

study.christ: Samuel tells Saul that God has torn the kingdom from him and given it to your neighbor who is better than you. That better neighbor is David, but David is only a signpost; the King who is truly and finally better is Jesus, the Son of David, whose obedience never fails. Saul's fatal error was to offer sacrifice in place of obedience, and Samuel's oracle exposes it: to obey is better than sacrifice. In Christ that opposition is healed, for His whole life was the perfect obedience that is itself the true and acceptable sacrifice; He said, I have come to do Your will, O God, and by that will we are made holy, Hebrews 10:9-10. Saul is rejected because he rejected God's word; Jesus is the beloved Son of whom the Father says, listen to Him, because He always did what pleased the Father, John 8:29. The tragedy of the rejected king teaches us to long for, and to rest in, the obedient King whose throne will never be torn away.
study.god: This chapter reveals God as holy, just, and utterly serious about obedience. He executes a long-announced judgment on a persistently evil people, and He requires His king to obey His word fully, not selectively. He sees straight through Saul's religious cover and blame-shifting, declaring that a listening, obedient heart pleases Him more than the fattest sacrifice. He is deeply engaged with His world, so that Scripture can speak of His regret and grief over Saul's sin, and yet He is the Glory of Israel who does not lie or change His mind, unchanging in His character and purposes even as He responds truly to human evil. He is sovereign, tearing the kingdom from the disobedient king and giving it to a better one, and faithful to His redemptive purpose even in the act of judgment. Such a God is to be feared, obeyed, and trusted, for He is both perfectly holy and perfectly faithful.

crossReferences.note: God requires not multiplied sacrifices but to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him, echoing obedience over sacrifice.
I desire mercy, not sacrifice, and the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings, the same principle Samuel declares.
Christ comes to do God's will, the true obedience and sacrifice that Saul's religion could never replace.
God sends Samuel to anoint David, the better neighbor to whom the kingdom is given.

study.oneStory: Saul's final rejection hangs on the great truth that God desires obedience above sacrifice and that to reject His word is to be rejected. The kingdom is torn from Saul and given to a better king, David, and through David the story runs on to Christ, the obedient King whose throne endures forever and whose obedience is the true sacrifice. The rejected king makes way for the King after God's own heart, and at last for the Son who always does the Father's will and cannot be rejected.

quiz.q: In what way did Saul disobey God's command against the Amalekites?

options.text: He spared King Agag and the best of the animals instead of devoting everything to destruction as commanded.
options.note: Verses 8 to 9; Saul kept back the best rather than fully obeying.

options.text: He kept the best sheep and cattle from the plunder.
options.note: True, but omits that he also spared Agag, the king, in direct disobedience.

options.text: He refused to fight the Amalekites at all.
options.note: He did fight and win; his sin was sparing the best rather than not fighting.

options.text: He obeyed God's command completely.
options.note: The chapter shows his partial obedience, which was in fact disobedience.

quiz.q: How did Saul respond when Samuel confronted him?

options.text: He claimed he had obeyed, blamed the troops, and said the best was kept to sacrifice to God.
options.note: Verses 13 to 21; Saul justified himself and shifted blame rather than repenting honestly.

options.text: He admitted the animals had been spared from the plunder.
options.note: He mentioned this, but framed it as obedience and blamed the troops, masking his rebellion.

options.text: He immediately confessed his full guilt without excuse.
options.note: His confession came later and only under pressure, mixed with excuses and concern for honor.

options.text: He said nothing and accepted the rebuke silently.
options.note: He argued that he had obeyed, rather than accepting the rebuke.

quiz.q: What is the central lesson of Samuel's oracle in verses 22 to 23?

options.text: To obey God's voice is better than sacrifice; rejecting His word brings rejection.
options.note: God desires obedient hearing above religious offerings.

options.text: God prefers obedience to burnt offerings and the fat of rams.
options.note: True, but omits the second half, that rejecting God's word leads to being rejected.

options.text: Sacrifices are worthless and should never be offered.
options.note: Sacrifice is not condemned in itself; it must not replace obedience.

options.text: God rewards those who offer the most sacrifices.
options.note: The opposite; God desires obedience more than sacrifice.

quiz.q: What did the tearing of Samuel's robe signify?

options.text: That God had torn the kingdom from Saul and given it to a better neighbor.
options.note: Verses 27 to 28; the torn robe enacts the torn kingdom.

options.text: That Saul was desperate to keep Samuel from leaving.
options.note: He did grab the robe in desperation, but the meaning was the torn kingdom.

options.text: That Samuel was angry and stormed away.
options.note: The tearing became a sign of judgment, not merely a display of anger.

options.text: That Saul would be restored to full favor.
options.note: The opposite; it signified the kingdom taken from him.

quiz.q: How does Saul's rejection point forward to Christ?

options.text: The kingdom passes to a better king, leading to Christ, the obedient King whose obedience is the true sacrifice.
options.note: Hebrews 10; where Saul substituted sacrifice for obedience, Christ perfectly obeyed.

options.text: It teaches that God values obedience in His leaders.
options.note: True, but stops short of Christ, the perfectly obedient King the chapter anticipates.

options.text: It shows that every king will ultimately be rejected by God.
options.note: The point is that a better King comes who cannot be rejected, not that all kings fail forever.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The better king and obedience over sacrifice clearly point to Jesus.

questions: Saul obeyed partly and called it full obedience. Where might you be settling for partial obedience to God and telling yourself it is enough?
Saul offered religious activity, sacrifice, in place of doing what God said. Are there ways you substitute church, giving, or busyness for simple obedience to God's word?
Saul blamed the troops and said he feared the people. When you disobey God, are you quick to shift blame or to name your fear of others rather than owning your sin?
God desires a listening, obedient heart above all sacrifice. What is one clear thing God has said that you need to simply obey this week?
The kingdom passed to a better King who always obeys the Father. How does resting in the obedience of Christ free you from trying to cover your disobedience with religion?

translationNotes.note: Devote to destruction renders cherem, the giving over of persons or things wholly to the LORD in judgment; nothing was to be taken as personal plunder. The term recurs through the chapter.
The source has an em dash before 200,000 foot soldiers; rendered here as a comma. The numbers are given as Saul musters the army at Telaim.
Obedience renders shama, to hear and obey, the word of the great command Hear, O Israel; obedience is set above sacrifice as what God truly desires.

The Glory of Israel does not lie or change His mind; this affirmation of God's constancy stands alongside the language of His regret in verses 11 and 35, describing His genuine grief over sin without fickleness.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 15:1-35. Verses 22, 23, and 33 are Hebrew poetry, rendered here as running lines. One em dash in verse 4 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
